gallery.text.1: Salvaged galvanised iron - presumably an old flue outer-casing. This was part of the formwork for the internal structure of the furnace.
gallery.text.2: Galvanised iron pipe to form tuyere for the furnace - also part of the formwork.
gallery.text.4: Furnace lid - the outer steel band was cold forged then welded to form the cylindrical structure in which the refractory lid was cast. A vent was formed with galvanised iron which was removed after the refractory was cast.
gallery.text.5: Side view of the refractory-based lid. Cross members of 1/4" steel rod were welded across the inside of the outer band to produce a reinforced structure.
gallery.text.6: A reclaimed paint-can was used as the outer shell of the furnace. Home-made refractory was cast into the shell to form the body of the furnace. Twelve years on, the original furnace is still in service.
gallery.text.7: Side view of teh paint-can shell - Galvanised iron was pop-rivetted to the shell for reinforcing to maintain the positin of the tuyere.
gallery.text.8: Furnace body with lid in place.
gallery.text.9: The furnace is quite heavy - a salvaged trolley was used to very good effect to enable the furnace to be wheeled around with relative ease.
gallery.text.10: A load of aluminum being melted in a makeshift, steel crucible that was made from 4" x 1/16" quare steel pipe.
